title: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
one_line_summary: Swiss multidisciplinary guideline providing consensus recommendations on epidemiology, risk stratification, diagnosis, definitions, and antifungal management of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis in ICU patients, including those with influenza, COVID-19, COPD, and liver disease.
key_pearls: IPA incidence in general ICU is <1%, but rises to **5–10%** in intermediate-risk groups (severe COVID-19, COPD, liver disease) and **>10%** in high-risk groups (severe influenza, prolonged neutropenia without prophylaxis).
Serum GM screening is **recommended** for high-risk hematologic cancer patients (A I), but has limited sensitivity for CAPA (**10–20%**) and should trigger bronchoscopy if positive.
Probable IPA in non-immunocompromised ICU patients requires deterioration plus new infiltrates AND **BALF GM ≥1.0 ODI**, positive BALF culture, or positive serum/BALF PCR (twice).

Isavuconazole is favored over voriconazole in selected **critically** ill patients due to better pharmacokinetic and safety profile (fewer drug–drug interactions, no QTc prolongation, no cyclodextrin accumulation).
Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is **recommended** for all patients on voriconazole (target trough **1.5–5 mg/L**) and posaconazole (target trough **>1 mg/L** for therapy), measured from day 5.

For proven/probable IPA without EORTC-MSGERC host criteria, minimum AFT duration is **4–6 weeks**; with host criteria, **6–12 weeks**, guided by clinical/radiological/microbiological response and ICU discharge.

Posaconazole prophylaxis is **recommended** in high-risk hematologic cancer patients (A I) but not in severe influenza due to early IAPA onset; it may be considered in severe COVID-19 if local CAPA incidence is **≥10%** (C II).
consensus_method: Modified ESCMID grading system (SoR A–D, QoE I–III) with ≥75% panel approval during plenary voting; working groups performed independent literature review.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- IPA in general ICU population has overall low incidence (<1%) except in specific subgroups stratified as low risk (1%–5%), intermediate risk (5%–10%), and high risk (>10%) (Table 1).
- High-risk category includes hematologic cancer with prolonged neutropenia (≥10 days) not receiving mould-active prophylaxis, allogeneic HCT without prophylaxis, and severe influenza.
- Intermediate-risk category includes lung/heart transplantation, severe acute/chronic liver disease, severe COPD, chronic immunosuppressive therapy, severe COVID-19, and cumulative low-risk conditions.
- Additional risk factors increasing IPA risk: high illness severity scores, intensive supportive care (prolonged mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, ECMO, RRT), antibiotic use, and immunomodulatory therapies (corticosteroids, IL-6 inhibitors).
- IAPA pooled incidence in hospitalized patients is **11%** (95% CI 6%–18%), rising to **20%–30%** in **critically** ill; about **75%** of IAPA patients have no classical immunosuppression.
- CAPA pooled incidence is **10%** with wide variability (0%–47%); most patients are not immunocompromised (**93%**) but receive steroids/IL-6 inhibitors (**70%**).
- Overall **mortality**: general ICU IPA **55%–>90%**; IAPA **30%–50%**; CAPA **40%–60%** in meta-analyses.

recommendations.statement: ICU patients with hematologic conditions classified within the high-risk category should undergo serial serum GM screening (A I).

For other high-risk categories, a marginal recommendation for screening is proposed (C II); if applied, use serum GM in severe influenza, or NBR samples (GM, fungal culture, or PCR) in order of preference.
Screening frequency should be every 3 to 4 days (e.g., twice weekly); a negative test does not rule out IPA and a positive test should trigger bronchoscopy.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Chest CT should be performed in any patient with worsening respiratory conditions without clear explanation (A III).
- Classical IPA findings (nodules, halo sign) are present in ≤25% and ≤12% of ICU cases, respectively; cavitary lesions in **10%–30%** (up to 73% in one study).
- Most frequent pattern (>70%) is non-specific consolidation or ground-glass opacities; bronchiectasis reported in **40%–80%** of CAPA.
- Bronchoscopy is indicated in all ICU patients (low/intermediate/high risk) with new radiological abnormality without alternative explanation (A II).
- Bronchoscopy is also indicated after positive Aspergillus test in serum (GM) or NBR samples (culture, GM, PCR) (A II).
- BALF should be tested with direct microscopy (A II), fungal culture (A II), GM testing (A II), and Aspergillus PCR (A II); their conjunction improves diagnostic accuracy.

recommendations.statement: Lung CT-scan should be performed in any patient with worsening respiratory conditions (A III).
All ICU patients (at low, intermediate or high risk for IPA) with a new radiological abnormality in the absence of alternative explanation should undergo bronchoscopy (A II).
Bronchoscopy is also indicated in case of a positive test for Aspergillus detection in serum (GM) or NBR samples (culture, GM, PCR) (A II).
BALF samples should be tested using direct microscopy (A II), fungal culture (A II), GM testing (A II) and Aspergillus PCR (A II).

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- For immunocompromised hosts fulfilling EORTC-MSGERC host criteria, use EORTC-MSGERC definitions.
- For non-immunocompromised ICU patients, a distinct classification is proposed (Table 3).
- Proven IPA requires direct observation of hyphae consistent with Aspergillus spp. and signs of tissue invasion, or positive culture/PCR from normally sterile tissue.
- Probable IPA requires host criteria (severe influenza, severe COVID-19, exacerbated severe COPD GOLD 3/4, severe acute/chronic liver disease, other respiratory viral infection, burns >35% TBSA) plus clinical/radiological criteria (deterioration, new infiltrates, or tracheobronchitis signs at bronchoscopy) plus microbiological criteria: GM ≥0.5 ODI in serum OR GM ≥1.0 ODI in BALF OR positive BALF culture OR positive Aspergillus PCR in serum (twice) OR positive PCR in serum and BALF.
- Possible IPA requires host and clinical/radiological criteria plus positive culture in NBR OR GM ≥4.5 in NBR OR GM ≥1.2 in NBR (twice) OR GM ≥1.2 and positive PCR in NBR.

recommendations.statement: For immunocompromised hosts, use EORTC-MSGERC criteria. For patients who do not fulfill EORTC-MSGERC host criteria, adapted definitions of IPA in ICU are proposed (Table 3).

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Patients fulfilling criteria of probable/proven IPA or Aspergillus tracheobronchitis should be treated with AFT as soon as possible (A II).
- Patients with possible IPA or clinical suspicion should undergo further diagnostic work-up (bronchoscopy) as soon as possible; AFT may be considered if bronchoscopy is not feasible/delayed or in case of persisting (≥2 consecutive) positive NBR results (A III).
- Isavuconazole or voriconazole are **recommended** as first-line therapy (A I for hematologic cancer patients, A II for other ICU patients).
- Isavuconazole may be preferred in selected **critically** ill patients due to better pharmacokinetic/safety profile (less variable exposure, fewer DDIs, no cyclodextrin component, less neurological/hepatic toxicity, no QTc prolongation).
- Liposomal amphotericin B (L-AMB) and posaconazole are acceptable alternatives (B II, B I/II respectively).
- Echinocandins can be considered in combination therapy for selected cases (C I/III) but should be avoided as monotherapy unless no alternative option.
- For suspected/proven azole resistance, L-AMB is first-line (A II); novel antifungals (fosmanogepix, olorofim) may be considered if available (B III).

recommendations.statement: Patients fulfilling criteria of probable or proven IPA or Aspergillus tracheobronchitis should be treated with AFT as soon as possible (A II).

Patients with criteria of possible IPA or clinical suspicion should undergo further diagnostic work-up (bronchoscopy) as soon as possible; AFT should be considered if bronchoscopy is not feasible/delayed or in case of persisting (≥2 consecutive) positive NBR results (A III).

Isavuconazole or voriconazole are **recommended** as first-line therapy for IPA in ICU patients (A I for patients with hematologic cancer, A II for other ICU patients). Isavuconazole might be preferred in selected **critically** ill patients.

Liposomal amphotericin B and posaconazole are acceptable alternatives (B II, B I/II respectively). Echinocandins can be considered in combination therapy for selected cases (C I/III) but should be avoided as monotherapy.
For suspected or documented azole resistance, liposomal amphotericin B is first-line agent (A II); novel antifungal drugs (fosmanogepix, olorofim) may be considered if available (B III).

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- In case of worsening clinical/radiological signs despite AFT for >7 days, repeat bronchoscopy and search for alternative explanation (e.g., insufficient drug exposure, alternative diagnosis).
- Switch of antifungal drug classes (from triazoles to L-AMB, or inversely) or combination therapies including an echinocandin represent preferred options.
- Change of antifungal within same class (mainly triazoles) should be restricted to situations where failure is attributed to insufficient drug exposure or toxicity (C III).
- Echinocandin monotherapy should be avoided unless no other option (C II/III).
- Adjunctive interventions: nebulized L-AMB (B III) mainly for Aspergillus tracheobronchitis; bronchoscopic debridement (C III); surgery (C III) for large lung lesions or extra-pulmonary lesions; arterial embolization (C III) for pseudoaneurysms/hemoptysis; immunomodulatory drugs (interferon gamma, G-CSF) (C III) for refractory IPA.
- Continuation of short-term corticosteroid therapy should be reassessed individually; benefit demonstrated for CAPA but not **recommended** in IAPA.

recommendations.statement: Switch of antifungal drug classes (from triazoles to L-AMB, or inversely) or combination therapies including an echinocandin represent preferred options for refractory IPA (A II/III).
Nebulized L-AMB may be considered as adjunctive therapy mainly in Aspergillus tracheobronchitis (B III).
Bronchoscopic debridement (C III), surgery (C III), arterial embolization (C III), and immunomodulatory drugs (C III) can be considered for selected cases.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring and Dosing Adjustments
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- TDM is **recommended** for all patients receiving voriconazole (A II) and posaconazole (A II); target trough: voriconazole **1.5–5 mg/L**, posaconazole >**0.7 mg**/L (prophylaxis) / >**1 mg**/L (therapy), measured from day 5.
- Isavuconazole TDM is not routinely **recommended** but may be considered in selected cases (ECMO, obesity, severe liver dysfunction) (C II); target trough >**2 mg**/L based on population PK.
- TDM for echinocandins is not routinely **recommended** but may be considered in selected **critically** ill patients to ensure adequate exposure (C III).
- In severe liver impairment, voriconazole clearance is affected (favor alternative); isavuconazole safe in mild/moderate impairment without dose adjustment; caspofungin clearance decreased in moderate/severe (consider anidulafungin).
- For ECMO patients, some circuit loss expected for triazoles but exposure usually not **significantly** affected; initial dosing adjustment not warranted but TDM **recommended**.
- In obese patients, dose L-AMB and voriconazole based on adjusted body weight (max **100 kg**); posaconazole increase to **400 mg** q24h if weight >**140 kg**; echinocandin dose increase may be considered if >**80 kg**.
- Oral administration via nasogastric tube is possible: crushed tablets (voriconazole, posaconazole), opened capsules (isavuconazole), or intravenous solution (isavuconazole).

recommendations.statement: TDM is **recommended** for all patients receiving voriconazole (A II) and posaconazole (A II); target trough concentrations: voriconazole 1.5–**5 mg**/L, posaconazole >**0.7 mg**/L (prophylaxis) / >**1 mg**/L (therapy), measured from day 5.
Isavuconazole TDM is not routinely **recommended** but may be considered in selected cases (C II); target trough >**2 mg**/L.
TDM for echinocandins is not routinely **recommended** and is not available in most centers; may be considered in selected **critically** ill patients (C III).

In patients with severe liver impairment, alternative AFT should be considered; isavuconazole safe in mild/moderate impairment without dose adjustment; anidulafungin preferred over caspofungin in moderate/severe impairment.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Follow-up chest CT is **recommended** at 4–6 weeks from start of AFT or earlier in case of lack of clinical improvement (A III).
- Monitoring of serum GM is **recommended** once weekly if initial value was positive (≥0.5 ODI) until negative (A II).
- Duration of AFT: minimum 6–12 weeks in patients with EORTC-MSGERC host criteria (B III); minimum 4–6 weeks in patients without host criteria (B III), provided favorable clinical/radiological/microbiological response, mechanical ventilation discontinued, and ICU discharge.
- In patients with possible IPA, earlier discontinuation of AFT (<4 weeks) can be considered in selected situations.
- Safety monitoring: hepatic tests twice during first week then once weekly (A III); serum creatinine and potassium 3 times weekly for L-AMB (A III); ECG QT interval at initiation, after 2–3 days, and at every change of medications with potential QT effect (A III).
- Antifungal prophylaxis with posaconazole is **recommended** in high-risk hematologic cancer patients (prolonged neutropenia or allogeneic HCT with severe GvHD) (A I).
- Prophylaxis is generally not **recommended** in other ICU patients; may be considered in severe COVID-19 in setting of high CAPA incidence (≥10%) (C II); not **recommended** in severe influenza due to early IAPA onset (D I).
- Nebulized L-AMB can be administered for prevention of IPA in lung transplant recipients during immediate post-transplantation period (B II).
- Close adherence to local infection prevention and control protocols should be followed to avoid spore contamination (A III).

recommendations.statement: Follow-up chest CT is **recommended** at 4–6 weeks from start of AFT or earlier in case of lack of clinical improvement (A III).
Monitoring of serum GM is **recommended** once weekly if the initial value was positive (≥0.5 ODI) until it turns negative (A II).
In patients with proven or probable IPA, minimum duration of AFT is 4–6 weeks without EORTC-MSGERC host criteria and 6–12 weeks with host criteria (B III).
Antifungal prophylaxis with posaconazole is **recommended** in high-risk hematologic cancer patients (prolonged neutropenia or allogeneic HCT with severe GvHD) (A I).

Prophylaxis with posaconazole or nebulized L-AMB may be considered in severe COVID-19 patients in a setting of high CAPA incidence (≥10%) (C II). Prophylaxis is not **recommended** in severe influenza (D I).
Nebulized L-AMB can be administered for the prevention of IPA in lung transplant recipients during the immediate post-transplantation period (B II).
Close adherence to local infection prevention and control protocols should be followed in ICU to avoid spore contamination (A III).

bedside_protocol.title: Risk Stratification on ICU Admission
bedside_protocol.action: Classify patient into low (1%–5%), intermediate (5%–10%), or high (>10%) risk category for IPA based on underlying conditions (Table 1) and presence of additional risk factors (high illness severity scores, intensive supportive care, antibiotic/immunomodulatory therapies).

bedside_protocol.title: Screening Strategy
bedside_protocol.action: For high-risk hematologic cancer patients, perform serial serum GM screening every 3–4 days. For other high-risk categories, consider screening with serum GM (severe influenza) or NBR samples (GM, fungal culture, or PCR). A positive test triggers bronchoscopy.

bedside_protocol.title: Diagnostic Work-up for Clinical/Radiological Deterioration
bedside_protocol.action: Perform chest CT in any patient with worsening respiratory conditions without clear explanation. If new pulmonary infiltrates without alternative diagnosis or any positive Aspergillus test in non-invasive samples, perform bronchoscopy with BALF for direct microscopy, fungal culture, GM, and Aspergillus PCR.

bedside_protocol.title: Classification and Treatment Initiation
bedside_protocol.action: Classify as proven, probable, or possible IPA using Table 3 criteria. For proven/probable IPA or Aspergillus tracheobronchitis, start AFT immediately (isavuconazole or voriconazole first-line). For possible IPA, complete bronchoscopy; if not feasible or delayed, consider AFT and reassess later.

bedside_protocol.title: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring and Dose Adjustment
bedside_protocol.action: Perform TDM for voriconazole and posaconazole from day5 (target troughs: voriconazole 1.5–5 mg/L, posaconazole >1 mg/L). Adjust dosing for organ dysfunction, ECMO, obesity, and nasogastric administration according to Table5. Monitor hepatic tests, renal function, potassium, and ECG QT interval.

bedside_protocol.title: Follow-up and Duration
bedside_protocol.action: Monitor serum GM weekly if initially positive. Perform follow-up chest CT at 4–6 weeks. Treat for minimum 4–6 weeks (without host criteria) or 6–12 weeks (with EORT C-MSGER C criteria), guided by clinical/radiological/microbiological response and ICU discharge.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: - Multidisciplinary panel covering infectious diseases, intensive care, pulmonology, microbiology, radiology, and pathology. - Adapted to Swiss epidemiology and healthcare system with explicit risk stratification tables. - Detailed practical algorithms for diagnostic work-up and therapeutic choices. - Comprehensive TDM and dosing adjustment recommendations for ICU-specific scenarios. Limitations: - Recommendations based largely on observational cohort studies and expert consensus due to lack of randomized trials in ICU populations. - Incidence data and diagnostic test performance derived from heterogeneous studies with variable definitions. - Limited evidence for many adjunctive interventions and salvage therapies. - Funding from pharmaceutical companies for logistical support, though without involvement in recommendation development or manuscript review.
